content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5662                322-5660              322-5901              322-6441              322-6444

May 5, 1980

Christopher A. Lee
14189 River Road
P.O. Box 673
Walnut Grove, CA 95804

Dear Mr. Lee:

Ted Prim, who is now the Executive Director of this agency,
asked me to respond to your letter of April 1, 1980.

As the Fair Political Practices Commission is not the code
reviewing body for any city officials other than the city council
(see Government Code Section 82011(a)), we do not as a rule main-
tain copies of Conflict of Interest Codes which cover other city
employees, such as planning directors. The obligation for a public
official to disqualify himself or herself arises not only from a
Conflict of Interest Code, but also directly from the pro-
visions of the Political Reform Act itself. See Government Code
Sections 87100 and 87102. I have enclosed for your convenience
a copy of the Political Reform Act, as amended to January 1, 1980,
copies of two FPPC regulations, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Sections 18700
and 18702, and a copy of the formal opinion request by Tom Thorner,
FPPC Opinions 198 (No. 75-089, 1975). This should provide some
guidance to you in interpreting Sections 87100 and 87103 of the Act.

Note, however, that in general the conflict of interest pro-
visions of the Political Reform Act regulate what a person can do
in his or her public capacity depending on the effects on his or
her private financial interests. These provisions do not restrict
what a person can do after leaving government service.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter or
would like additional materials, please do not hesitate to contact
us again.

Sincerely,

Sarah T. Cameron
Deputy Chief for
Conflicts of Interest
Legal Division

STC:nrw
Enclosures

CHRISTOPHER LEE
ATTORNEY AT LAW

WALNUT GROVE—
(OLD BANK BUILDING)
14186 RIVER ROAD
WALNUT GROVE, CA 95690
PHONE (916) 776-1731
SACRAMENTO—
665 1903
MOBILE PHONE (916) 778-0294

SACRAMENTO—
916 NINTH STREET
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA
PHONE (916) 446-5475

April 1, 1980

Please Reply To Walnut Grove


California Fair Political
Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Post Office Box 806
Sacramento, California 25804

Attention: Division Chief Ted Prim

Re: Conflict of Interest Code
City of Martinez

Dear Mr. Prim:

On behalf of the Fountainhead Garden Homeowners Association,
a planned unit development located in the City of Martinez
and incorporated under the laws of the State of California,
I have been directed by said Board of Directors to
communicate with the Commission regarding a possible
conflict of interest and violations thereof by the former
planning director of the City of Martinez, Mr. Barry
Whittaker.

Would you please forward to my office at your earliest
convenience a copy of said conflict of interest criteria.

It has come to our attention that Mr. Whittaker, shortly
after resigning as planning director, went to work for a
prominent developer in the City of Martinez, to wit, Security
Owners, Inc. Thereafter, it is our information and our
firsthand observation that Mr. Whittaker commenced

California Fair Political
Practices Commission
Attention: Division Chief Ted Prim
April 1, 1980
Page Two


representing said developer before meetings of the Martinez
Planning Commission and the Martinez City Council regarding
planned specifications for housing developments in the
City of Martinez.

I would be most appreciative on behalf of the Board of
Directors if this information could be conveyed to us so
we may make a determination of whether or not to proceed
in this matter further.

Thank you for your courtesy and consideration in this
regard.

Very truly yours,

CHRISTOPHER A. LEE

CAL:mgh

cc: Jim Carroll

sections.question: Would you please forward to my office at your earliest convenience a copy of said conflict of interest criteria. It has come to our attention that Mr. Whittaker, shortly after resigning as planning director, went to work for a prominent developer in the City of Martinez, to wit, Security Owners, Inc. Thereafter, it is our information and our firsthand observation that Mr. Whittaker commenced representing said developer before meetings of the Martinez Planning Commission and the Martinez City Council regarding planned specifications for housing developments in the City of Martinez.
sections.conclusion: As the Fair Political Practices Commission is not the code reviewing body for any city officials other than the city council, we do not as a rule maintain copies of Conflict of Interest Codes which cover other city employees, such as planning directors. The obligation for a public official to disqualify himself or herself arises not only from the Conflict of Interest Code; but also directly from the provisions of the Political Reform Act itself. See Government Code Sections 87100 and 87102. Note, however, that in general the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act regulate what a person can do in his or her public capacity depending on the effects on his or her private financial interests. These provisions do not restrict what a person can do after leaving government service.
sections.question_synthetic: Does a former city planning director violate the Conflict of Interest Code when, shortly after resigning, he begins working for and representing a developer before the Planning Commission and City Council on housing development matters?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC does not maintain Conflict of Interest Codes for non-council city officials like planning directors, but disqualification obligations arise from the Political Reform Act itself. Generally, the Act regulates conduct while in public service and does not restrict what a person can do after leaving government.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both incoming request (Lee's April 1, 1980 letter) and FPPC response (Cameron's May 5, 1980 letter). Classified as advice_letter based on the FPPC response component. No formal QUESTION and CONCLUSION sections present; content extracted from letter body. Incoming request discusses potential conflict of interest by former planning director post-departure from government.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Would you please forward to my office at your earliest convenience a copy of said conflict of interest criteria. It has come to our attention that Mr. Whittaker, shortly after resigning as planning director, went to work for a prominent developer in the City of Martinez, to wit, Security Owners, Inc. Thereafter, it is our information and our firsthand observation that Mr. Whittaker commenced representing said developer before meetings of the Martinez Planning Commission and the Martinez City Council regarding planned specifications for housing developments in the City of Martinez.

CONCLUSION: As the Fair Political Practices Commission is not the code reviewing body for any city officials other than the city council, we do not as a rule maintain copies of Conflict of Interest Codes which cover other city employees, such as planning directors. The obligation for a public official to disqualify himself or herself arises not only from the Conflict of Interest Code; but also directly from the provisions of the Political Reform Act itself. See Government Code Sections 87100 and 87102. Note, however, that in general the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act regulate what a person can do in his or her public capacity depending on the effects on his or her private financial interests. These provisions do not restrict what a person can do after leaving government service.

embedding.summary: An attorney inquires on behalf of a homeowners association about potential conflicts of interest by a former Martinez planning director who subsequently represented a developer before city boards. The FPPC responds that while it does not review codes for non-council officials, the Political Reform Act provisions apply directly, and such Act does not restrict post-government employment activities.
